food_subgroup: Noodle soup set meal with beverage and side

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Mee Tai Bak Soup Set. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup) is used as the closest parent category. The set-meal composite nature (noodle soup + beverage + side) is not captured by any single FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Mee Tai Bak or its Singaporean set-meal composite form.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for Mee Tai Bak Soup Set. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific Singaporean noodle dish.

main_ingredients: mee tai bak (silver needle rice noodles)
kopi or teh (coffee or tea beverage)
side dish (e.g. soft-boiled egg or toast)
allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce or side)

serving.description: 1 set (noodle soup bowl + 1 large beverage + 1 side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set comprises approximately 350 g of mee tai bak soup (noodles, broth, and toppings), a large beverage of approximately 150 g (kopi or teh), and a side dish of approximately 50 g (soft-boiled egg or toast), totalling roughly 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Tai Bak Soup (Silver Needle Noodle Soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy rice noodle and pork broth components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for the full Mee Tai Bak Soup Set composite. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for mee tai bak soup (plain noodle soup base) and USDA SR Legacy data for rice noodles (USDA FDC ID 168936) and pork broth components. The set-meal composite (noodle soup + beverage + side) is estimated by weighting component contributions to a per-100 g basis across the full 550 g serving. Values represent a moderate-confidence estimate for the composite set meal. The beverage (kopi/teh with condensed milk) and side (soft-boiled egg or toast) contribute additional energy, fat, and sugar. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to Mee Tai Bak Soup Set composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Tai Bak Soup Set. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary glycemic driver in this dish. The soup broth, beverage (kopi/teh with condensed milk), and side (soft-boiled egg or toast) contribute additional carbohydrates that modestly influence the composite GL. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 78.1 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.4, rounded to 41.5. This is a medium-confidence analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Mee Tai Bak Soup Set due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,705 mg per full set serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth and avoiding added soy sauce is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles and sweetened beverage component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with Mee Tai Bak Soup Set as the rice noodle base has a GI of approximately 53 (low-medium boundary) and the set's sweetened kopi or teh beverage adds significant sugar load; substituting the beverage with unsweetened tea or water and controlling portion size of noodles can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from broth and set components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of Mee Tai Bak Soup Set due to its combined sodium load from the soup broth and potential saturated fat contribution from the side dish and beverage (condensed milk in kopi/teh); choosing a clear broth option, requesting less condensed milk, and limiting frequency of consumption aligns with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Tai Bak Soup Set (米台目汤套餐) is a popular Singaporean hawker and kopitiam set meal centred on mee tai bak, also known as silver needle noodles or rat tail noodles — short, thick, tapered rice-flour noodles with a smooth, chewy texture and a characteristic pointed tip resembling a rat's tail. The noodles are served in a clear, savoury pork or chicken broth enriched with minced pork, fish balls, or sliced meat, garnished with bean sprouts, spring onions, and fried shallots. The set meal format, as served at establishments such as Toast Box, includes one large beverage (typically kopi — traditional Singaporean coffee brewed with robusta beans and sweetened with condensed milk — or teh, a similarly prepared milk tea) and one side dish (commonly a soft-boiled egg or a slice of kaya toast). Nutritionally, the composite set provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 5.2 g, low fat of 2.8 g, and a carbohydrate content of 14.2 g per 100 g. The full set serving of approximately 550 g delivers roughly 539 kcal. The sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, driven primarily by the soup broth. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low-medium boundary per University of Sydney GI Database), though the sweetened beverage component raises the overall glycemic load of the set. Mee tai bak noodles are made from rice flour and water, making them naturally gluten-light, though cross-contamination and soy-sauce additions are common. This dish is a staple of Singaporean breakfast and lunch culture, widely available at kopitiam chains and hawker centres across the island.
common_user_queries: Is mee tai bak soup healthy?
How many calories in a mee tai bak soup set?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the Mee Tai Bak Soup Set composite. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo mee tai bak soup data and USDA SR Legacy rice noodle and broth component data, weighted across the full set-meal serving. The composite nature of the set (noodle soup + beverage + side) introduces estimation uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this food category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this composite dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in rice noodle and clear broth preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. The set-meal composite includes a beverage (kopi or teh with condensed milk) and a side (soft-boiled egg or kaya toast); nutrient values reflect a weighted per-100 g estimate across the full ~550 g set. 6. Halal status is listed as false because Toast Box uses pork-based broth in some preparations; consumers should verify with the specific outlet. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mee Tai Bak Soup Set was found in approved sources. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 78.1 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.5. 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific Singaporean set meal.
